text: The bedside assistant injects vasopressin, causing visible blanching of the tissue. The surgeon uses monopolar scissors, known as hot shears, to make a vertical incision, with the cutting energy indicated on the screen. The incision is extended to reach the correct plane of the fibroid. The fibroid begins to emerge as the surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar grasper in the left hand, although bipolar energy is not currently applied.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm for retraction while the third and first arms perform active surgery, reaching the submucosal part of the fibroid. Tiny feeding vessels are visible, and slight energy is applied to the vascular connections without affecting the myometrium. The robotic arms allow precise movements with minimal stretching of the abdominal wall, reducing postoperative pain.

The fibroid is nearly removed from the myoma bed and is set aside. The surgeon prepares to begin suturing. The camera is controlled by the surgeon, and the robotic instruments are manipulated with precision, ensuring safety by using either the monopolar or bipolar energy as needed.

The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close the U-triangle in two layers, demonstrating precise wrist movements. The needle passes through the loop, achieving tight tissue approximation and closure of the dead space. This is the first layer of suturing for the deep and large fibroid.

The surgeon completes the first layer of suturing using a continuous V-lock suture technique, ensuring minimal exposure of the myometrium and suture to the peritoneal cavity. The bedside assistant removes the needle and provides a new suture for the second layer, which is also applied in a continuous fashion using base wall sutures with inside-out and outside-in technique.

The surgeon completes suturing and proceeds with bipolar coagulation and cutting of the right and left fallopian tubes using scissors. The specimen is received through morcellation, with visible activity on the screen indicating the energized instruments.
